summary: Focused fan-out review of the soup-sim implementation plan before building

strengths: The plan's Self-Review traceability matrix maps every major spec section to a task, and the mapping is mostly accurate: the in-code invariant lint (Task 1, test_lint_invariants.py) faithfully implements the spec §1 sender/recipient isolation requirement with a concrete regex over the addressing-blind layer set, and the oracle is correctly confined to metrics.py (Task 10).

Per-episode (not per-step) budget charging is correctly designed: Engine (Task 9) tracks open contact episodes {(i,j): entry_time}, settles once on episode end over the full duration, and the unit test test_per_episode_budget_caps_transfer directly guards spec §3's 'charge budget per contact episode, never per step' invariant.

Analytic dt-independent contact timing (Task 3, contact_interval) is implemented with the correct relative-motion closest-approach quadratic and includes the spec-mandated tangential fly-through test, which is the right way to satisfy §3/§4's dt-independence and missed-contact requirements.

RNG contract (Task 1) is solid and spec-faithful: single injected Generator, SeedSequence-derived substreams keyed by path, plus a grep/lint test forbidding module-global np.random.* matching §5 exactly.

The density-aware budget formula (Task 7) correctly composes all five §6 ingredients in one place — t_setup floor (short-contact-transfers-zero test), contention divisor 1/(1+α·n_local), p_fail Bernoulli thinning, and whole-blob quantization — and exposes a bound counter for the binding diagnostic.

findings.title: GATE measures the wrong quantity: delivery=0.5 midpoint does NOT land at the percolation threshold d≈4.51
findings.issue: The gating test in Task 12 (test_static_torus_recovers_percolation_threshold) asserts the density where delivery_mean first reaches 0.5 lies in [3.5, 5.5] and calls that 'the theoretical ~4.51'. This conflates two distinct quantities. The continuum-percolation threshold d_c≈4.51 is where the GIANT COMPONENT first EMERGES (giant-component fraction S goes from 0 to positive), confirmed in the literature (critical mean degree 4.508–4.515). But the test's own delivery model is 'a message delivers iff src and dst are in the SAME connected component', i.e. delivery_ratio ≈ Σ (component_size/N)^2, which at threshold is dominated by S^2 where S = 1 − exp(−d·S). At d=4.51, S is just emerging and small, so delivery is near ZERO, not 0.5. Delivery only reaches ~0.5 (S≈0.71) at a substantially HIGHER mean degree, roughly d≈6–7. So a CORRECT simulator that genuinely recovers the 4.51 cliff will produce a delivery=0.5 midpoint of ~6–7 and FAIL this gate; conversely, code that passes [3.5,5.5] is not actually validating against 4.51. The gate — which spec §2.1(A)/§9/§10 make the make-or-break validation that 'gates everything downstream' — is mis-specified against the very theory it cites.
findings.fix: Validate the threshold the spec actually names: the GIANT-COMPONENT KNEE, not the delivery=0.5 point. Concretely, in the B=inf/TTL=inf/buffer=inf static-torus harness compute the largest-connected-component fraction S(d) directly (a union-find / BFS over neighbor_pairs at the static placement) and assert S(d) transitions from ~0 to clearly positive across d≈4.51 within tolerance (e.g. S(4.0)<0.1 and S(5.5)>0.3, with the steepest-rise point near 4.51). If the team wants to keep a delivery-based assertion, either (a) assert delivery tracks the analytic S(d)^2 curve rather than hard-coding a 0.5 crossing at 4.51, or (b) move the [3.5,5.5] window onto the giant-component emergence point and stop labeling the delivery=0.5 midpoint as 4.51. Update spec §2.1/§9/§10 wording to say 'giant-component / connectivity knee near 4.51', which is what §2.1 already hints ('giant-component / delivery knee').

findings.title: Mean-degree axis definition risks a factor-of-2 / boundary mismatch with the 4.51 convention
findings.issue: Spec §2.2 and Task 5/Task 12 define the density control as d = N·πr²/(W·H) and Task 12 inverts it as N = round(density·W·H/(πr²)). The theoretical 4.51 is the EXPECTED DEGREE = λ·πr² where λ is the Poisson intensity and an edge exists when two nodes are within r (Gilbert disk model, connection radius r). The plan's d = N·πr²/(WH) = λ·πr² matches IF and only IF 'in range' uses center-to-center distance ≤ r — which geometry.in_range does (dist2 <= r*r), good. But there is a classic trap: some literature states the threshold for the model where two disks of radius r OVERLAP (i.e. connection range 2r), giving the same 4.51 in terms of expected degree but a different N for a given r. The plan never states which convention its r is, and the empirical-degree cross-check (mean_degree = 2*len(pairs)/n, Task 5) is the only safeguard. Task 5's static mean-degree test only asserts within 25% of n·πr²/(WH), which is too loose to catch a 2x or boundary-induced bias.
findings.fix: Pin the convention explicitly in geometry/config docstrings: 'edge iff center-to-center dist ≤ r; expected degree d = λπr² matches the 4.51 continuum-percolation constant.' Tighten Task 5's static_mean_degree test on a TORUS (no boundary deficit) to assert agreement within ~5% at large N (e.g. n=5000), since on a torus the empirical mean degree must equal n·πr²/(WH) with no edge effects. Keep the 25% tolerance only for the walls case. Add an assertion in the gate harness that empirical_mean_degree ≈ nominal density before trusting the curve (spec §2.2 demands plotting against empirical degree — the plan computes it but never gates on the nominal≈empirical match).

findings.title: Static-uniform PRIMARY probe is specified as 'uniform/Poisson' but the plan only does fixed-count uniform — fixed-N is NOT the Poisson ensemble that 4.51 is defined for
findings.issue: Spec §2.1(A) says 'STATIC uniform/Poisson placement on a torus ... matches infinite-plane theory'. The 4.51 continuum-percolation constant is defined for a POISSON point process (random N ~ Poisson(λWH)), not a fixed-N binomial point process. Task 5 implements 'fixed uniform positions' (deterministic N = round(...)), i.e. a binomial process. For finite systems these differ slightly in the degree distribution and component statistics near threshold, and the spec explicitly listed Poisson as an option for exactly this reason. The plan silently chose fixed-N and never notes the deviation. With R=20 reps and finite N this likely sits inside tolerance, but it is undocumented drift from a signed-off spec clause and could bias the recovered threshold if N is small.
findings.fix: Either (a) draw N ~ rng.poisson(λ·W·H) per replication in the gate harness (cheap, removes the ensemble mismatch and matches theory exactly), or (b) explicitly document in the README caveats and in Task 5/12 that a fixed-N binomial point process is used as an approximation to the Poisson ensemble and state the expected direction/magnitude of bias. Option (a) is preferred since the spec named Poisson and the gate is the load-bearing validation.

findings.title: RWP stationary init is reduced to a 'document it' hand-wave, not implemented — spec §2.3 requires perfect-stationary init OR enforced warm-up discard, AND a passing stationarity check before any curve is accepted
findings.issue: Spec §2.3 is mandatory: 'initialize positions+speeds from the stationary RWP distribution (perfect init) OR discard a detected warm-up window' AND 'a stationarity sanity check ... must pass before a curve is accepted.' Task 5 Step 3 instead says: 'place positions uniformly (acceptable approx; document center-concentration is then a transient removed by warm-up) OR run an internal burn-in.' This (a) does NOT sample from the stationary RWP spatial distribution (uniform init is precisely the non-stationary start RWP is known to relax away from — the center-of-mass concentration), and (b) leans on warm-up to fix it but Task 11 run_one never asserts the stationarity check passed; it just runs warmup time. The stationarity sanity check (§2.3, first-half vs second-half contact-rate within tolerance) appears only as a single unit test in Task 5 (test_rwp_no_speed_decay_first_vs_second_half) on a manual run — it is NOT wired into the scenario as a per-curve GATE that rejects a non-stationary curve. So the spec's 'must pass before a curve is accepted' acceptance gate has no task.
findings.fix: Add to Task 11 (run_one / sweep): after warm-up, compute the stationarity diagnostic (first-half vs second-half mean-degree or contact-rate over the measurement window) and FAIL/flag the replication if it exceeds tolerance, recording a 'stationary_ok' boolean in each CSV row (Task 12). For init, either implement true stationary RWP sampling (the known closed-form/ rejection sampler for the RWP spatial density) or keep uniform init but make the warm-up-discard mandatory and verified. Since static-uniform is the PRIMARY probe and RWP is a labeled overlay, the team may scope true-stationary RWP as overlay-only, but the acceptance gate must still exist for any RWP curve.

findings.title: Fair-chance delivery denominator in the plan does not match the spec's definition (uses drain, not in-window TTL headroom)
findings.issue: Spec §2.4 defines: 'fair-chance cohort = messages whose creation_ts leaves ≥ one max-TTL of IN-WINDOW sim time' — i.e. the message must have a full max-TTL of MEASUREMENT-WINDOW time after injection to have a fair chance, explicitly 'excludes right-censored end-of-run messages'. Task 10's Metrics.delivery_ratio uses the predicate 'inject_time + ttl <= measure_end + drain'. Because the spec mandates a drain of ≥ one max-TTL AFTER the last injection (§2.4) precisely so deliveries are not right-censored, the predicate inject_time + ttl <= measure_end + drain is satisfied by essentially EVERY message when drain ≥ max_TTL — making the fair-chance filter a no-op and defeating its purpose. The spec's intent is the opposite: a message injected late in the window, even with full drain, had less ELAPSED real opportunity only if you measure against in-window time; but the operative censoring concern the spec encodes is in-window TTL headroom. The plan's formula and the spec's wording diverge, and the plan's KAT (Step 1 'denominator excludes right-censored') will pass against the plan's own wrong predicate, masking the drift.
findings.fix: Reconcile with spec §2.4 explicitly. Given the default workload is a SINGLE cohort injected at warm-up end (§2.4), all messages share one inject_time and the denominator is trivially all-or-nothing — so this matters most for the optional swept-rate workload. Implement the spec's predicate literally: fair-chance iff (creation_ts + max_ttl) <= warmup_end + measure_window (one max-TTL of in-window time), NOT measure_end + drain. Then the drain (≥ max-TTL after last injection) exists to let those fair-chance messages actually complete without censoring. Add a KAT that, with a late-injected message lacking in-window TTL headroom, the message is EXCLUDED from the denominator (the current Step-1 test does not pin this because the predicate is too permissive).

findings.title: dt-convergence check (spec §4) has no task or test
findings.issue: Spec §4 requires a 'dt-convergence check in the validation plan: halving dt changes delivery ratio and mean contact duration by < tolerance' and 'Log dt + a missed-contact estimate in the CSV.' Spec §9 lists 'dt-convergence' as a unit test. The plan's Self-Review claims '§4 dt/CFL+per-episode → Tasks 1,3,9', but Task 1 only does the CFL startup assertion (v_max·dt ≤ r/4), Task 3 does analytic timing, and Task 9 does per-episode budgeting — NONE of them implements or tests a dt-halving convergence check, and no task logs a missed-contact estimate to the CSV. This is a named §9 unit test and a §4 requirement with zero coverage.
findings.fix: Add an explicit dt-convergence test (e.g. in test_engine.py or test_scenario.py): run a fixed scenario at dt and dt/2, assert |delivery(dt) − delivery(dt/2)| < tol and |mean_contact_duration(dt) − ...(dt/2)| < tol. Add a 'missed_contact_estimate' column to the CSV manifest in Task 12 (e.g. count of contact_intervals shorter than dt, which a step-based detector would have missed) and a 'dt' column (the manifest already includes dt via asdict, but the missed-contact estimate is new). Note: because contacts are analytically timed, delivery should be near-perfectly dt-invariant — which is exactly why the test is cheap and worth having as a regression guard.

findings.title: Binding-constraint diagnostic is computed but the spec's 'if ~0 across the sweep, say so / abstraction is decorative' decision is not implemented
findings.issue: Spec §6(6) requires not just computing bound_fraction but a DIAGNOSTIC interpretation: '≥ 3 B levels (binding/marginal/non-binding) + ... the fraction of contacts where the budget actually bound. If ~0 across the sweep, we're measuring contact-graph percolation, not airtime-limited delivery, and the abstraction is decorative (say so).' The plan threads bound_fraction through Task 7 (counter), Task 9, Task 11 (returned), and Task 12 (CSV column) — good plumbing — but (a) there is no task to SWEEP B at ≥3 levels (binding/marginal/non-binding); the only sweep is over density. The CLI preset 'static-cliff' and sweep() only vary density. (b) There is no logic/report assertion that flags 'budget never bound → decorative' to the README/output. So a §6 must-have (the B-sweep) and its interpretive output are missing.
findings.fix: Add to Task 11/12: a B-level sweep dimension (at least 3 buffer/throughput settings chosen to be binding / marginal / non-binding), and in report.py emit a one-line diagnostic when max(bound_fraction) across the sweep is below a small threshold (e.g. < 0.05): 'budget never bound — measuring contact-graph percolation, not airtime; airtime abstraction decorative for these params.' Add a preset (e.g. --preset airtime-sweep) so the one-command run covers it. This is the only place the spec's co-headline cost-curve (§1) becomes actionable.

findings.title: Co-headline circulation/airtime COST curve (spec §1, DoD #2) is under-specified — only overhead_ratio is produced, no airtime-cost vs density plot
findings.issue: Spec §1 makes circulation/airtime cost a CO-EQUAL headline output ('(b) circulation/airtime cost — because for a pure-flooding system the cost curve is co-equal with delivery'), and DoD #2 requires 'plots for BOTH delivery (mean + 95% CI) AND circulation/airtime cost vs density.' The plan produces transmissions (Task 9) and overhead_ratio = transmissions/delivered (Task 10), and Task 12 plot() is mentioned but its Step 3 only describes write_csv/plot generically and the gate/CLI example only plots/uses the delivery curve. There is no explicit task step to compute and plot total airtime (bytes on air, or transmissions, or per-node airtime) vs density as a second headline figure, and overhead_ratio (transmissions/delivered) is a RATIO that blows up to infinity at the sparse end where delivered→0 — it is not the same as the absolute circulation/airtime cost the spec wants.
findings.fix: Make Task 12's plot() explicitly emit TWO figures (or a twin-axis): delivery_mean+Wilson CI vs density, AND an absolute circulation/airtime-cost curve vs density (total transmissions and/or total bytes-on-air and/or mean per-node airtime). Add these as CSV columns. Keep overhead_ratio as a derived column but do not let it stand in for the cost curve (guard the divide-by-zero when delivered==0). Add a test that the cost curve is monotone-ish / non-empty across the sweep.

findings.title: Engine samples n_local_contenders 'at contact midpoint' — a modeling choice the spec ties to 'peers within r at contact time'; midpoint can bias the airtime model
findings.issue: Spec §6(1) defines n_local_contenders = 'peers within r at contact time'. Task 9 implements this by sampling contenders 'at contact midpoint'. For a long co-location episode the local crowd changes over the episode; sampling a single midpoint snapshot is a reasonable simplification but is an UNSTATED abstraction with an unstated bias direction, and the spec (§1, DoD #5) requires each abstraction to be logged with its direction of bias in the fidelity table. Midpoint sampling tends to UNDERSTATE contention if the crowd grows toward episode end (venue filling), making delivery an even looser upper bound — consistent with the spec's 'every number is an upper bound' framing, but it must be recorded.
findings.fix: Keep midpoint sampling (cheap, defensible) but record it in the README fidelity-to-parent table (Task 12 Step 5) as an explicit abstraction with bias direction (optimistic: single-snapshot contention can understate true time-varying contention). Optionally add a config flag to sample contenders at entry vs midpoint vs max-over-episode for a sensitivity check, since §6(1) also asks to 'sweep/sensitivity-test α' — the contender count interacts directly with α.

findings.title: Half-duplex / per-direction-vs-shared budget decision (spec §3) is left unresolved in the plan
findings.issue: Spec §3 (Exchange) requires a decision: 'Budget per-direction or shared (justified vs BLE half-duplex).' BLE is half-duplex, so a single shared budget per episode is the physically-honest default and the spec asks for an explicit justification. Task 9 says 'exchange both directions respecting budget' and Task 7's blobs_transferable takes a single duration — it is ambiguous whether the budget is charged once (shared) or once per direction (effectively doubling airtime). This ambiguity directly affects the cost curve and the upper-bound claim (per-direction = 2x optimistic on throughput for half-duplex).
findings.fix: Decide and document: for BLE half-duplex, charge ONE shared budget over the episode duration split across both directions' payloads (not a full budget each way). Make Task 9's settle step compute k_total = blobs_transferable(duration, ...) once and allocate it across the two directions' missing sets (e.g. interleave or split). Add a budget test asserting total blobs transferred in an episode <= the single-episode budget regardless of direction count, and note the half-duplex justification in the README.

reviews.lens: TDD-TESTS — are the plan's tests sufficient to catch real bugs, and are any tests WRONG?
strengths: The gating test exists at all and is wired as a real integration test (Task 12, test_static_torus_recovers_percolation_threshold) rather than a unit stub — the instinct to validate the density axis against an external ground truth (~4.51) before trusting any downstream curve is exactly right, and continuum percolation does indeed depend only on mean degree d=lambda*pi*r^2, so the chosen control parameter is theory-correct.

Determinism contract is concrete and testable: a single injected numpy Generator, SeedSequence.spawn substreams, and a lint test (Task 1, test_no_module_global_rng) that greps for np.random.* / random.seed. The substream test (test_rng_substreams_independent_and_deterministic) correctly checks both reproducibility (same path -> identical) and independence (different path -> different).

The invariant lint (Task 1, test_engine_layers_are_addressing_blind) is a genuinely good cheap guard: a regex for sender|recipient across model/engine/policies enforces inv-2/inv-4 mechanically, and Task 10 isolates the oracle to metrics.py.

Geometry tests are tight and meaningful: torus minimum-image is tested with a real wrap case (dist2((1,1),(99,1))==4 on a 100-torus), and in_range is tested at the inclusive boundary (5.0 -> True, 5.001 -> False), pinning the dist2 <= r*r convention. The analytic contact_interval has a head-on known-answer ([8,10]) and a sub-dt tangential fly-by case.

The cell-list test is the correct shape: brute-force-equivalence over random points for BOTH walls and torus (Task 4, test_celllist_equals_bruteforce_random) — this is the right way to test a spatial index.

The fair-chance denominator is identified as a known-answer test target (Task 10) and the right-censoring rule (inject_time + ttl <= measure_end + drain) is stated, which protects against the most common DTN delivery-ratio inflation bug.

findings.title: GATING TEST IS WRONG: pairwise-delivery=0.5 measures P_inf^2, so the knee lands well ABOVE the 4.51 threshold and the 3.5-5.5 band is mislocated
findings.issue: Task 12's gate asserts the *mean pairwise delivery* over uniform-random distinct src/dst crosses 0.5 within [3.5, 5.5] and calls that the percolation threshold (~4.51). But with B=inf/TTL=inf/buffer=inf flooding on a static torus, a message delivers iff src and dst lie in the same connected component, so mean delivery = sum_i (s_i/N)^2 ~= P_inf(d)^2 (dominated by the giant component; small-component pairs are negligible). The percolation transition is CONTINUOUS: the giant-component fraction P_inf is 0 at d=4.51 and rises gradually above it (2D order-parameter exponent beta=5/36; Penrose: largest-component fraction -> P_inf). For mean delivery to reach 0.5 you need P_inf ~= sqrt(0.5) ~= 0.707, i.e. the giant component must hold ~71% of nodes. In the RGG node-connectivity model that happens around d ~= 6-7, NOT 4.51. At d=4.51 the test uses only N~574 nodes (d*W*H/(pi*r^2) with W=H=200,r=10); finite-size lifts the largest-component fraction off zero but P_inf^2 there is still ~0.1-0.25, far below 0.5. Net effect: on a CORRECT implementation the delivery midpoint lands near d~=6, outside [3.5,5.5] -> the gate fails good code; and if someone widens the band to make it pass, it 'validates' a knee that is not the percolation threshold — the exact 'confident, reproducible, and wrong' failure mode the spec's section 2.1 gate exists to prevent.
findings.fix: Stop using pairwise-delivery=0.5 as the percolation-threshold estimator. Pick a connectivity-specific knee locator: (a) PREFERRED — susceptibility peak: compute chi(d) = sum over all-but-largest components of s_i^2 / N (or mean size of the 2nd-largest component) and assert argmax_d chi is in [4.0, 5.2]; this peaks AT the threshold and is the textbook finite-size locator. Or (b) finite-size-scaling crossing of the largest-component fraction curves for two N values (assert crossing in [4.0,5.0]). If you insist on keeping a 'delivery' framing, change the metric to the giant-component FRACTION P_inf(d) = |largest component|/N and assert its emergence knee (e.g. the steepest-rise / inflection point of P_inf, or P_inf crossing a small value like 0.1-0.2 with N-extrapolation) lands near 4.51 — never P_inf^2 = 0.5. Document explicitly in the test that pairwise mean delivery is P_inf^2 and therefore unsuitable as a threshold estimator.

findings.title: Gate has no independent ground-truth cross-check (union-find), so it can pass on a buggy exchange that accidentally mimics the wrong curve
findings.issue: The gate runs the full engine/exchange/scenario stack and then compares an emergent number to ~4.51. There is no test asserting that, in the B=inf/TTL=inf/buffer=inf static regime, 'delivered(src,dst)' equals 'src and dst are in the same connected component' computed by an independent oracle. Any exchange bug (e.g. a contact missed because contacts are dt-sampled — see separate finding, or an off-by-one in episode settling) shifts the curve, and a loose [3.5,5.5] band can absorb the shift, so the gate gives false confidence. The spec section 9 calls for a percolation HARNESS that 'recovers' the threshold; recovery should be checked against an explicit reachability oracle, not just a band.
findings.fix: Add a known-answer test independent of degree: build a static torus placement, compute connected components via union-find over neighbor_pairs (or scipy-free BFS), and assert that the set of delivered (src,dst) pairs from a single unbounded-flooding run EXACTLY equals the set of same-component pairs. This pins the exchange/flooding semantics deterministically and lets the degree-sweep gate use the susceptibility-peak metric on top of a verified foundation.

findings.title: Analytic contact_interval (Task 3) is built and unit-tested but NOT wired into the engine — contacts are actually dt-sampled, so the dt-independence and tangential-fly-through claims are untested/false in the real pipeline
findings.issue: Task 9 Step 3 detects episodes by diffing neighbor_pairs once per dt step ('new pair = episode start; vanished pair = episode end'); entry_time and duration come from the dt grid. contact_interval is listed under Consumes but the algorithm never calls it. So (1) the analytic timing is dead code, (2) actual contact durations are quantized to dt and the per-episode budget is charged on a dt-quantized duration, and (3) any contact whose in-range window opens and closes between two dt samples (the fast tangential fly-through the spec explicitly wants to keep) is MISSED — precisely the 'free/lost delivery on a brief brush' bias section 3/4 say to eliminate. No test catches this: the only engine determinism test compares a run to itself, and there is NO dt-convergence test in Task 9 despite spec section 4 requiring 'halving dt changes delivery and mean contact duration by < tolerance'.
findings.fix: Wire contact_interval into the engine: per dt leg, for each candidate pair from the cell-list, call contact_interval(pa,va,pb,vb,r,t0,t0+dt,...) to get the true sub-interval; accumulate per-episode entry/exit across consecutive legs and charge the budget on the analytic duration. Add (a) a dt-convergence test: run the same scenario at dt and dt/2 and assert delivery_ratio and mean contact duration agree within tolerance; (b) a sub-dt fly-through engine test: two nodes whose in-range window is shorter than dt must still register a contact and (if budget allows) transfer. Without these the engine silently reverts to a dt-dependent contact graph.

findings.title: Eviction test lacks the property test and the same-timestamp tie-break case the spec requires
findings.issue: Spec section 9 demands 'eviction exact-victim (oldest cohort, never closest-to-TTL) + property test'. Task 6 provides only test_cap_never_exceeded_and_victim_is_oldest_cohort ('survivors are younger; len<=cap'). This does not (1) verify the randomized tie-break WITHIN a same-created_at oldest cohort (which is the only place rng is used in eviction, and a likely determinism/bias bug site), nor (2) include the property test, nor (3) assert the explicit NEGATIVE: that a blob close to TTL but young by creation is NEVER evicted in favor of an old-but-far-from-TTL blob (the exact anti-pattern parent section 9.5 forbids). A naive 'evict min(expires_at)' implementation would PASS the given test in many fixtures while violating the rule.
findings.fix: Add: (a) a same-timestamp tie test — fill the oldest cohort with several blobs sharing created_at, force one eviction, assert exactly one is dropped and which one is chosen is determined by the injected rng (reproducible across two same-seed buffers, different across seeds). (b) The forbidden-inversion known-answer test: buffer holds an OLD blob with lots of TTL remaining and a YOUNG blob about to expire; on overflow assert the OLD one is evicted (creation-order), proving it is NOT closest-to-TTL eviction. (c) A property/hypothesis-style test over random insert sequences asserting the invariant 'every evicted blob's created_at <= every survivor's created_at'.

findings.title: No byte-identical-CSV determinism test despite spec section 5 requiring it; only aggregate equality is tested
findings.issue: Spec section 5/9 require 'two same-seed runs produce byte-identical output CSV' and 'two cells run in swapped order are identical'. Task 11 Step 1 only mentions aggregate determinism ('same master_seed -> identical aggregate; reps in swapped order -> identical') and Task 12 produces the CSV but has no determinism assertion on it. Float formatting, dict/column ordering, or rng-substream ordering bugs can produce identical aggregates but non-identical CSV bytes (or vice versa), so the spec's strongest reproducibility guarantee is unverified end-to-end.
findings.fix: Add to Task 12 (or 11): write the sweep to two files from two same-seed invocations and assert the file BYTES are equal (open(p,'rb').read()). Add a swapped-rep-order test: run reps in order [0,1,2] and [2,1,0] and assert identical aggregates AND identical CSV — this is what proves SeedSequence.spawn order-independence actually holds through report.write_csv.

findings.title: Anti-resurrection seen-record test does not exercise 'past capacity' eviction-then-re-offer, the case the spec singles out
findings.issue: Spec section 9: 'seen-record anti-resurrection past capacity'. Task 6 gives test_no_resurrection_within_window (accept, evict/expire, re-offer -> RejectedSeen) and test_seen_window_aging_allows_after_window. Neither forces the seen-record itself to overflow/age its FIFO while the buffer is over capacity — i.e. the dangerous interaction where a blob is EVICTED for space (not expired) and then re-offered: if eviction forgets to write a seen-record entry, the blob resurrects and delivery_count > unique-recipient-first-hold (the spec's stated invariant). The current test says 'evict/expire' ambiguously; it must pin the evict-for-space path specifically.
findings.fix: Split into two tests: (a) EVICT-for-space then re-offer same id -> RejectedSeen (proves eviction records the id in the seen-record, not just expiry). (b) Fill the seen-record FIFO past its own implied capacity within the window and assert the oldest *still-within-window* ids are NOT aged out prematurely (FIFO-by-time, not FIFO-by-count). Also add the integration assertion the spec names: across a junk-flood run, delivery_count == count of unique (recipient-first-hold) events.

findings.title: Budget quantization/thinning tests miss the 1-blob boundary and rng-determinism of p_fail thinning
findings.issue: Task 7 tests duration<t_setup->0, more-contenders->fewer, 0.9-blob->0, and p_fail=1.0->0. Gaps: (1) the lower boundary that matters operationally — exactly-1.0-blob -> 1 and exactly-1.9 -> 1 (floor behavior at the knee), since the whole point of quantization is the sparse-end bias; (2) p_fail is a per-blob Bernoulli thinning applied via the injected rng, but there is no test that the thinning is reproducible under the same rng (determinism) and statistically correct for 0<p_fail<1 (e.g. p_fail=0.5 over many blobs ~ half survive within tolerance). A non-determinism or applied-before-quantize bug would slip through.
findings.fix: Add: test_quantize_boundary (time for exactly 1.0 and 1.9 blobs -> 1; 2.0 -> 2). Add test_pfail_deterministic_and_unbiased: same-seed rng gives identical thinning; over N>=1000 offered blobs with p_fail=0.5, surviving fraction in [0.45,0.55]. Confirm in a comment/test that p_fail thins AFTER floor-quantization (offered whole blobs), matching the formula in the interface.

findings.title: Tangential test passes only because offset 1.9 < r; an exactly-grazing (disc==0) contact is silently dropped — document or test the convention
findings.issue: contact_interval returns None when disc <= 0.0, so an exactly-tangential pass (single touch point, disc==0) yields no contact. The test test_tangential_flyby_detected uses offset 1.9 (disc>0) so it passes, but the spec phrase 'fast tangential fly-through' could be read to include the grazing limit. The measure-zero grazing case is a fine modeling choice, but it is currently neither documented as the convention nor tested, so a future refactor to disc < 0.0 would change behavior with no guard.
findings.fix: Either keep disc<=0 -> None and add a one-line test asserting the exactly-grazing case returns None as the documented convention (and note 'measure-zero, intentionally excluded'), or change to disc<0 and test that a grazing pass returns a zero-width touch that the engine treats as no transfer. Pick one and pin it with a test so the boundary is not refactor-fragile.

reviews.lens: BUILD-RISK — task ordering/dependencies and under-specification that could produce a wrong build
strengths: TDD spine is genuinely sound: every task is failing-test-first → implement → pass → commit, and the failing tests encode the load-bearing invariants (analytic tangential fly-through in Task 3, brute-force cell-list equivalence in Task 4, oldest-cohort eviction + no-resurrection in Task 6, t_setup-floor / quantization / p_fail in Task 7).

Dependency DAG is mostly correct and bottom-up: geometry (T2/T3) → cell-list (T4) → mobility (T5) → blob/buffer (T6) → budget (T7) → policies (T8) → engine (T9) → workload/metrics (T10) → scenario (T11) → report+gate (T12). Nothing is consumed before it is produced, with the one exception noted in findings.

Invariant guard is enforced mechanically and early (Task 1 lint: no module-global RNG; sender/recipient only in metrics), so the addressing-blind / sealed-sender invariants cannot silently rot as later tasks are built.

The percolation gate is placed last (Task 12) so it integrates the whole stack, and the spec's theoretical anchor (mean degree ~4.51) is correct — Monte-Carlo estimates put the continuum-percolation critical mean degree at 4.51218–4.51228, so the 3.5–5.5 acceptance band is appropriately generous.

Determinism contract is concrete and testable (SeedSequence.spawn substreams keyed by (density_idx, rep), byte-identical CSV, swap-order identical), which is exactly what makes a located cliff trustworthy.

findings.title: Static-torus delivery path produces ONE-HOP delivery, not component reachability — the gate cannot pass as designed (HIGHEST RISK)
findings.issue: The whole spec gates on §2.1(A): static + torus + B/TTL/buffer=∞ must reproduce delivery crossing 0.5 near mean-degree 4.51, where 'a message delivers iff src and dst are in the SAME connected component' (Task 12 Step 1 comment). But the Task 9 engine settles an exchange ONLY when a contact episode closes — 'vanished pair = episode end → settle exchange ... at run end settle all open episodes' (Task 9 Step 3). Under static mobility, nodes never move, so NO pair ever vanishes during the run; every edge is settled exactly once, all at run-end, in a single pass. A single settle is one round of one-hop exchange: a blob injected at A reaches A's direct neighbors only, never the 2-hop+ nodes in the same component. So measured delivery ≈ P(dst is a direct neighbor of src) = 1 - exp(-d) for mean degree d, which crosses 0.5 at d ≈ 0.69 — nowhere near 4.51, and it is NOT a percolation/component curve at all. Task 12 Step 3's claim 'component reachability emerges naturally from unbounded flooding' is the load-bearing hand-wave and it is FALSE for this engine. The gate at the 3.5–5.5 band will FAIL, and the predictable failure mode is an implementer hacking the test (widening the band, or special-casing static) to make the loop's gate go green — i.e. a confident, reproducible, WRONG cliff, exactly the outcome the spec was revised to prevent.
findings.fix: De-risk by making multi-hop propagation explicit and testing it BEFORE the gate. Two concrete options: (a) Engine fix — for the static case (and generally), iterate exchange to a fixpoint: re-settle contacts until no buffer changes in a full pass (or run measure_window for enough dt steps that flooding saturates), so transitive reachability actually happens. Add a Task-9 test 'three nodes in a line A-B-C (A,C out of range) → C eventually holds A's blob' that FAILS on a one-hop engine. (b) Reachability oracle for the validation regime — in the B=TTL=buffer=∞ static path, define delivery directly as same-connected-component on the neighbor graph (BFS/union-find over neighbor_pairs), and document it as the §2.1(A) idealization. Either way: add the explicit multi-hop test as a gate PREREQUISITE in Task 9, and delete the 'emerges naturally' phrasing. Recommend (a) because the dynamic sweep (regime B) needs real multi-hop flooding anyway, so a one-hop engine is wrong for the headline measurement too, not just the gate.

findings.title: Gate config runs ~1 dt step (measure_window=1.0, dt=1.0, drain=0.0) — even a correct multi-hop engine gets no time to flood
findings.issue: Task 12 Step 1 sets measure_window=1.0, dt=1.0, drain=0.0 with static mobility. That is a single step. Even after fixing finding #1 via per-step iterative flooding (option a), one dt step = at most one exchange round = one hop. Multi-hop saturation needs either fixpoint iteration within the settle, or many steps. With drain=0.0 and a fair-chance denominator requiring inject_time+ttl <= measure_end+drain, the interaction with ttl=1e9 also needs checking (see finding #5). As written, the gate config is internally inconsistent with the 'same connected component' semantics it asserts in its own comment.
findings.fix: If fixing via fixpoint iteration (finding #1 option a), the settle must loop to convergence regardless of step count, and a single-step run is then fine — but state that explicitly. If fixing via the component oracle (option b), the engine time loop is bypassed for this regime. Either way, add an assertion/comment in the gate that the static path reaches a flooding fixpoint, and pick measure_window/dt so CFL and the timeline contract are satisfied even though motion is zero. Add a unit test that the static gate result is invariant to measure_window and dt (it must be, if it is truly a component property).

findings.title: Contact episodes are never opened/closed correctly when neighbor_pairs is recomputed per step for the DYNAMIC regime — boundary cases drop or double-charge episodes
findings.issue: Task 9 opens an episode on 'new pair' and closes on 'vanished pair', diffing the per-step neighbor_pairs set. Three failure modes are unspecified: (1) A fly-through that enters AND exits within a single dt (pair present at neither step boundary) is detected by contact_interval (Task 3) but NEVER by the per-step set-diff, so it is silently dropped — directly contradicting §3's promise of dt-independent contacts and the Task 3 tangential-flyby test. The set-diff approach is fundamentally dt-DEPENDENT, defeating the analytic-timing investment. (2) A pair that is in-range at step k, out at k+1, back in at k+2 is charged as two episodes with a fabricated gap; whether that double-charges t_setup is unspecified. (3) Episode duration: is it (exit_step - entry_step)*dt, or the analytic contact_interval duration? Only the latter is dt-independent, but Task 9 says 'compute duration' without saying which. The plan computes contact_interval in Task 3 but Task 9 never says it is actually used to set episode duration — it may be dead code.
findings.fix: Specify the episode bookkeeping precisely in Task 9: drive episode open/close and DURATION from contact_interval over each leg, not from the neighbor_pairs set-diff. neighbor_pairs (cell-list) should only PRUNE candidate pairs to feed contact_interval; the analytic interval determines entry/exit/duration. Add Task 9 tests: (a) a sub-dt fly-through delivers/charges exactly one episode of the analytic duration; (b) halving dt leaves transmissions and mean contact duration within tolerance (the §4 dt-convergence check — currently promised in the spec but assigned to no task). Without this, the dt-convergence DoD is untestable.

findings.title: Wilson CI is statistically wrong for this design — trials within a replication are correlated, not independent Bernoulli
findings.issue: Task 11 reports a Wilson 95% CI, and the gate aggregates delivery across reps. Wilson assumes independent Bernoulli trials. Here each replication injects M=200–400 messages into ONE network realization; the per-message delivery outcomes within a replication are strongly correlated (they share the same component structure / contact graph). Pooling successes/trials = R*M into a single Wilson interval treats correlated outcomes as independent, badly understating the CI width — defeating §2.5's entire purpose ('a single seed cannot locate a cliff', non-overlapping CIs across the transition as an integration acceptance test in §9). The literature is explicit: the Wilson interval is not appropriate for clustered/correlated binary data. The unit of replication is the SEED, not the message.
findings.fix: Treat each replication's delivery RATIO as the observation. Compute the CI across the R per-replication ratios (t-interval on the R means, or bootstrap over reps), not a Wilson interval over R*M pooled messages. If a Wilson interval is wanted for a single-replication proportion, keep it strictly intra-replication and never pool across the shared-realization messages. Update Task 11's wilson_ci test to also include an across-reps CI function, and make the §9 'non-overlapping CIs across the transition' integration test use the across-reps interval.

findings.title: density→N mapping is introduced in Task 12 but consumed by the gate, and rounding bias at small density is unaddressed
findings.issue: sweep() is built in Task 11, but the density→N mapping it needs (N = round(density * w*h/(pi r^2))) is deferred to Task 12 Step 3. So Task 11's sweep tests (sweep determinism) run against a sweep that cannot yet map density→N — either Task 11 is testing a stub or the dependency is mis-ordered. Also, with width=height=200, r=10: cell area pi*100=314, area=40000, so N = round(d*127.3). At d=2 → N=255, d=8 → N=1019: fine. But the mapping uses the NOMINAL density; §2.2 requires plotting against EMPIRICAL mean degree too, and round() introduces a quantization wobble in the realized density that matters near the cliff where the 0.5 crossing is read off. The gate reads mid via 'first row with delivery_mean>=0.5' (a step function on coarse 13-point grid), which is far cruncher than the logistic fit the DoD #3 mandates.
findings.fix: Move the density→N mapping into Task 11 where sweep is defined (it is sweep's core responsibility), so Task 11 tests exercise the real mapping. In the gate (Task 12), read the midpoint from logistic_cliff() (already built in Task 11, mandated by DoD #3), NOT from the first-crossing row — the first-crossing read is grid-resolution-limited and will jitter the 3.5–5.5 assertion across seeds. Record and also assert against the EMPIRICAL mean degree (§2.2) so round()-induced density error does not silently shift the measured cliff.

findings.title: RWP stationary-init shortcut contradicts §2.3 and the documented center-concentration; the stationarity sanity check is promised but unassigned
findings.issue: Task 5 Step 3 offers 'place positions uniformly (acceptable approx; document center-concentration is then a transient removed by warm-up) OR run an internal burn-in.' The RWP stationary distribution is provably non-uniform and center-concentrated (Bettstetter/Resta/Santi); uniform init is the WRONG stationary distribution, and the spec §2.3 explicitly requires init from the stationary distribution OR a discarded warm-up window validated by a first-half-vs-second-half stationarity sanity check. The plan's Task 5 test mentions 'first-half vs second-half mean contact count within tolerance' but no task acceptance ties the curve's acceptance to that check passing, and §2.2 requires plotting delivery vs EMPIRICAL mean degree precisely because nominal != realized under RWP center-concentration. If the implementer picks uniform-init with warmup=0.0 (the gate config) the RWP overlay silently measures a non-stationary transient.
findings.fix: In Task 5, require the burn-in path (run n waypoint legs before t=0) as the default for RWP, OR require warmup>0 with the stationarity sanity check as a hard precondition that run_one asserts before returning an RWP curve. Make the stationarity check (§2.3) an explicit acceptance in Task 5 and a guard in Task 11 run_one (raise/flag if first-half vs second-half mean-degree diverge beyond tolerance). Ensure §2.2's empirical-mean-degree plotting is computed over the steady-state window only, and that RWP curves are plotted against empirical, not nominal, degree.

findings.title: Per-episode budget recomputing the cell-list every dt step at 50k nodes × R reps × density points × seeds is a likely perf wall with no profiling/scale task
findings.issue: §8 names a 50k-node × R-replication sweep and keeps a Rust hot-path 'only if profiling demands.' The engine (Task 9) recomputes neighbor_pairs (cell-list) EVERY dt step. For the DYNAMIC regime, steps = (warmup+measure+drain)/dt; with TTL~600 and CFL dt ~ r/(4*v_max), step counts run into the thousands. 50k nodes * thousands of steps * 20-30 reps * ~13 density points is on the order of 1e9-1e10 cell-list builds. There is NO task that profiles this or sets a scale budget, and no Python-vectorization requirement on neighbor_pairs (Task 4 only requires brute-force equivalence on N=200). A pure-Python per-pair loop in neighbor_pairs (as the Task 4 prose 'iterate each cell's points' implies) will not survive 50k.
findings.fix: Add an explicit perf/scale task (or a sub-step in Task 4/Task 9): vectorize neighbor_pairs with numpy (bucket via argsort/bincount, candidate pairs via broadcasting per cell) and add a benchmark test asserting a wall-clock budget for one 50k-node step. Decide the Rust trigger with a concrete number, not a vibe. Also: for the STATIC regime the cell-list need only be built ONCE (positions never change) — special-case that to avoid 99% of the gate's work. Document the dynamic-sweep scale actually targeted in v1 (it may be far below 50k); 50k may belong to a fast-follow.

findings.title: Engine settle on a recomputed-per-step pair set can re-charge t_setup on the same physical contact, biasing the sparse end the spec is most sensitive about
findings.issue: §6.2 makes t_setup a per-episode floor specifically to kill 'free delivery on a 1-second brush' at the sparse end — the exact regime that locates the cliff. But the per-step set-diff episode model (finding #3 case 2) can fragment one physical contact into multiple episodes across step boundaries (numerical jitter, or genuine in-out-in), each paying t_setup again, OR conversely treat one long static co-location as a single run-end episode paying t_setup once. These two regimes (static vs dynamic) then charge airtime asymmetrically, contaminating the cross-regime comparison that §2.1 says is the whole point ('see whether a delivery drop is connectivity or airtime/TTL').
findings.fix: Tie episode identity to the analytic contact_interval per leg (finding #3 fix), so t_setup is charged exactly once per physical entry→exit regardless of dt or mobility mode. Add a Task 7/Task 9 test: a single uninterrupted contact spanning K dt steps pays t_setup exactly once; the binding-constraint/bound_fraction diagnostic (§6.6) is computed per physical episode. This keeps the static and dynamic airtime accounting comparable.

findings.title: numpy-only logistic fit is feasible but the planned 'least-squares on logit' is fragile at the 0/1 saturated tails that dominate a cliff
findings.issue: Task 11 proposes 'scipy-free logistic via numpy least-squares on logit, or simple bisection on a monotone fit.' Fitting by transforming ratios to logit then linear least-squares blows up at ratio=0 and ratio=1 (logit = ±inf), which is exactly where most sweep points sit (a cliff is mostly saturated at the ends). Dropping the 0/1 points to avoid inf discards most of the data and biases the midpoint. DoD #3 requires a falsifiable midpoint WITH a CI from replications, so the fit must be robust.
findings.fix: Implement a small Gauss-Newton / Levenberg-Marquardt logistic MLE in pure numpy (2 params: midpoint, slope) using the per-replication success counts as binomial observations (proper likelihood handles 0/1 cleanly), not a logit-linear regression on the means. Get the midpoint CI by bootstrapping over the R replications (reuses finding #4's per-rep observations). Add a Task 11 test that the fit recovers a synthetic sigmoid midpoint even when the sampled grid is mostly 0s and 1s.

mustFix.title: THE percolation gate is mis-designed three ways at once — fix it before anything else, or the build's only ground-truth check is worthless
mustFix.fix: All three lenses independently flagged the Task 12 gate as the make-or-break failure. It has three compounding defects that must ALL be fixed together: (1) WRONG ENGINE SEMANTICS (BUILD-RISK, highest risk): in static mobility no neighbor pair ever 'vanishes', so the Task 9 settle-on-episode-end loop runs exactly one exchange round = ONE HOP. Measured delivery becomes P(dst is a direct neighbor)=1-exp(-d), crossing 0.5 at d~0.69 — not a component curve at all. The Task 12 Step 3 claim 'component reachability emerges naturally from unbounded flooding' is FALSE for this engine. Fix: make multi-hop explicit — for the static B=TTL=buffer=inf regime iterate the exchange to a fixpoint (re-settle until no buffer changes in a full pass), and add a Task-9 PREREQUISITE test 'A-B-C line, A and C out of range -> C eventually holds A's blob' that fails a one-hop engine. The dynamic sweep needs real multi-hop flooding anyway, so this is not gate-only. (2) WRONG ESTIMATOR (SPEC-COVERAGE + TDD-TESTS, same finding): even with multi-hop fixed, mean pairwise delivery = sum_i (s_i/N)^2 ~= P_inf^2, which only reaches 0.5 when P_inf~0.71, i.e. mean degree ~6-7 — NOT 4.51. The continuum constant 4.51 is the GIANT-COMPONENT EMERGENCE point, not the delivery=0.5 midpoint. So a correct simulator lands the 0.5 crossing well outside [3.5,5.5] and the gate fails good code; widening the band to pass 'validates' a non-threshold. Fix: stop using delivery=0.5 as the threshold estimator. Preferred locator = susceptibility peak: compute chi(d)=sum over all-but-largest components of s_i^2/N and assert argmax_d chi in [4.0,5.2] (textbook finite-size threshold locator), OR assert the largest-component fraction S(d)=|largest|/N emergence knee near 4.51 (e.g. S(4.0)<0.1, S(5.5)>0.3). Update spec/plan wording to 'giant-component / connectivity knee near 4.51'. (3) NO INDEPENDENT ORACLE (TDD-TESTS): the gate compares an emergent number from the full stack to a band with no cross-check, so any exchange bug a loose band absorbs gives false confidence. Fix: add a known-answer test that, in the static unbounded regime, the set of delivered (src,dst) pairs EXACTLY equals same-connected-component pairs computed by an independent union-find/BFS over neighbor_pairs. Build this verified foundation first, then run the susceptibility-peak sweep on top. SINGLE HIGHEST-RISK THING TO GET RIGHT: the failure mode here is an implementer hacking the band to make a green-loop gate pass, producing exactly the 'confident, reproducible, and wrong' cliff the spec was revised to prevent. The gate must be re-specified before any code is written.

mustFix.title: Gate config runs effectively ONE dt step (measure_window=1.0, dt=1.0, drain=0.0) — no time to flood even after the engine fix
mustFix.fix: Task 12 Step 1 sets measure_window=1.0, dt=1.0, drain=0.0 with static mobility = a single step = one exchange round. This is internally inconsistent with the 'same connected component' semantics its own comment asserts. After fixing the one-hop bug via fixpoint iteration, the settle must loop to convergence regardless of step count (then a single-step run is fine — state this explicitly). If instead the static regime uses the union-find component oracle directly, the time loop is bypassed for this regime. Either way add a unit test that the static gate result is INVARIANT to measure_window and dt (it must be, if it is truly a component property).

mustFix.title: contact_interval (Task 3) is dead code — the engine is dt-sampled, so dt-independence and tangential fly-through are untested/false in the real pipeline; no dt-convergence check exists
mustFix.fix: All three lenses flag this. Task 9 detects episodes by diffing the per-step neighbor_pairs set (new pair=start, vanished=end) and derives entry_time/duration from the dt grid; contact_interval is listed under Consumes but never called. Consequences: (a) analytic timing is dead code; (b) per-episode budget is charged on a dt-quantized duration; (c) a contact whose in-range window opens AND closes between two dt samples — the fast tangential fly-through the Task 3 test specifically validates — is silently MISSED, reintroducing the dt-dependent contact graph spec sections 3/4 exist to eliminate; (d) a pair that goes in->out->in across step boundaries is charged as two episodes paying t_setup twice (sparse-end airtime bias, the exact regime that locates the cliff). Fix: in Task 9, use the cell-list neighbor_pairs ONLY to prune candidate pairs, then drive episode open/close/DURATION from contact_interval over each dt leg, accumulating entry/exit across consecutive legs; charge t_setup exactly ONCE per physical entry->exit and the budget on the analytic duration. Add tests: (1) a sub-dt fly-through registers exactly one episode of the analytic duration and transfers if budget allows; (2) DT-CONVERGENCE (spec section 4 / section 9, currently assigned to NO task): run the same scenario at dt and dt/2, assert |delivery(dt)-delivery(dt/2)|<tol and |mean_contact_duration(dt)-...(dt/2)|<tol; (3) a single uninterrupted contact spanning K dt steps pays t_setup exactly once. Add 'dt' and 'missed_contact_estimate' columns to the Task 12 CSV manifest.

mustFix.title: Wilson CI is statistically wrong — message outcomes within one replication are correlated, not independent Bernoulli; the unit of replication is the SEED, not the message
mustFix.fix: Task 11 pools successes/trials = R*M into a single Wilson interval. Each replication injects M=200-400 messages into ONE network realization, so per-message outcomes share the same component/contact structure and are strongly correlated. Pooling treats them as independent and badly understates CI width — defeating section 2.5's whole purpose (a single seed cannot locate a cliff; non-overlapping CIs across the transition is the section 9 integration acceptance test). Fix: treat each replication's delivery RATIO as the observation; compute the CI across the R per-replication ratios (t-interval on the R means, or bootstrap over reps), NOT a Wilson interval over pooled messages. Keep Wilson only for a single intra-replication proportion if wanted, never pooled across the shared-realization messages. Update Task 11's CI test to include an across-reps interval and make the section 9 'non-overlapping CIs' integration test use it. This also supplies the per-rep observations needed for a robust bootstrap midpoint CI in DoD #3.

mustFix.title: RWP stationarity acceptance gate (spec section 2.3) is hand-waved — uniform init is the wrong stationary distribution and the 'must pass before a curve is accepted' check is wired to no task
mustFix.fix: Spec section 2.3 is mandatory: init from the stationary RWP distribution OR a discarded+validated warm-up, AND a first-half-vs-second-half stationarity sanity check that MUST pass before a curve is accepted. Task 5 Step 3 instead offers uniform init (provably non-stationary, center-concentrated per Bettstetter/Resta/Santi) and leans on warm-up, but the gate config uses warmup=0.0 and run_one (Task 11) never asserts the stationarity check passed — it exists only as a manual unit test. Fix: in Task 5 require the burn-in path (run n waypoint legs before t=0) as the RWP default, OR require warmup>0 with the stationarity check as a hard precondition. In Task 11 run_one, after warm-up compute the diagnostic (first-half vs second-half mean-degree/contact-rate over the measurement window) and FAIL/flag the replication if it exceeds tolerance, recording a 'stationary_ok' boolean per CSV row. Since static-uniform is the PRIMARY probe, true-stationary RWP may be scoped as overlay-only, but the acceptance gate must still exist for any RWP curve, and section 2.2 empirical-mean-degree must be computed over the steady-state window only and RWP plotted against empirical (not nominal) degree.

mustFix.title: Mean-degree convention unpinned and empirical-vs-nominal agreement is never gated — risks a factor-of-2 / boundary bias against the 4.51 axis
mustFix.fix: Spec section 2.2 / Task 5 / Task 12 define d = N*pi*r^2/(W*H). This matches the 4.51 constant ONLY if 'in range' uses center-to-center distance <= r (the classic trap: the overlapping-disk model uses range 2r, same expected-degree number but different N). geometry.in_range does use dist2<=r*r (correct), but the convention is never documented and Task 5's static mean-degree test allows 25% slop — too loose to catch a 2x or boundary bias. Fix: pin in geometry/config docstrings 'edge iff center-to-center dist <= r; expected degree d = lambda*pi*r^2 matches the 4.51 continuum constant'. Tighten Task 5's static mean-degree test ON A TORUS to ~5% at large N (e.g. n=5000, where empirical must equal n*pi*r^2/(WH) with no edge effects); keep 25% only for walls. Add a gate-harness assertion that empirical_mean_degree ~= nominal density before trusting the curve (section 2.2 demands plotting against empirical degree — the plan computes it but never gates on the match). Also move the density->N mapping out of Task 12 into Task 11 where sweep is defined and tested, and read the gate midpoint from logistic_cliff() not the grid-resolution-limited 'first row >= 0.5' (which jitters the band across seeds).

mustFix.title: Fair-chance delivery denominator predicate diverges from spec section 2.4 — uses drain headroom, making the filter a no-op
mustFix.fix: Spec section 2.4 defines fair-chance cohort = messages whose creation_ts leaves >= one max-TTL of IN-WINDOW sim time, explicitly excluding right-censored end-of-run messages. Task 10 uses 'inject_time + ttl <= measure_end + drain'. With the mandated drain >= max_TTL after last injection, this predicate is satisfied by essentially every message — the fair-chance filter becomes a no-op, and the planned KAT passes against the wrong predicate, masking the drift. Fix: implement the spec predicate literally — fair-chance iff (creation_ts + max_ttl) <= warmup_end + measure_window (one max-TTL of in-window time), NOT measure_end + drain; the drain then exists so those fair-chance messages can complete uncensored. Add a KAT that a late-injected message lacking in-window TTL headroom is EXCLUDED from the denominator (the current Step-1 test does not pin this because the predicate is too permissive). Note: with the default single-cohort-at-warmup workload this is all-or-nothing, so it matters most for the optional swept-rate workload — but the predicate must still be correct.

shouldConsider: Eviction tests are too weak (TDD-medium): a naive 'evict min(expires_at)' would pass the single given test. Add (a) a same-created_at tie-break test proving the choice is rng-determined (reproducible same-seed, different cross-seed); (b) the forbidden-inversion KAT — an OLD blob with lots of TTL left vs a YOUNG blob about to expire, on overflow the OLD one must be evicted (proves NOT closest-to-TTL); (c) a property test over random insert sequences asserting every evicted blob's created_at <= every survivor's created_at.

Anti-resurrection test does not exercise the evict-FOR-SPACE-then-re-offer path the spec singles out (TDD-medium). Split into: (a) evict-for-space then re-offer same id -> RejectedSeen (proves eviction writes a seen-record, not just expiry); (b) seen-record FIFO ages by TIME not COUNT within the window; plus the integration assertion delivery_count == count of unique recipient-first-hold events across a junk-flood run.

No byte-identical-CSV determinism test despite spec section 5 requiring it (TDD-medium). Add: two same-seed sweep invocations produce byte-identical files (open(p,'rb').read() equal), and reps run in order [0,1,2] vs [2,1,0] produce identical aggregates AND identical CSV (proves SeedSequence.spawn order-independence holds through report.write_csv).

Co-headline circulation/airtime COST curve (spec section 1, DoD #2) is under-specified — only overhead_ratio is produced, which blows up at the sparse end where delivered->0. Make Task 12 plot() emit TWO figures: delivery_mean + across-reps CI vs density, AND an absolute cost curve (total transmissions and/or bytes-on-air and/or mean per-node airtime) vs density, with divide-by-zero guarded. Add as CSV columns.

B-level sweep + binding-constraint diagnostic (spec section 6.6) is plumbed but never exercised. Only density is swept. Add a >=3-level B sweep (binding/marginal/non-binding) and an 'airtime-sweep' preset, and have report.py emit a one-line diagnostic when max(bound_fraction) < ~0.05: 'budget never bound — measuring contact-graph percolation, not airtime; abstraction decorative for these params.'

Static-uniform primary probe uses fixed-N (binomial point process), but 4.51 is defined for a Poisson process (TDD/SPEC-medium). Either draw N ~ rng.poisson(lambda*W*H) per replication in the gate harness (cheap, removes the ensemble mismatch), or explicitly document the fixed-N approximation and expected bias in the README and Task 5/12.

Performance/scale has no task (BUILD-medium). The dynamic engine rebuilds the cell-list every dt step; 50k nodes x thousands of steps x ~20-30 reps x ~13 densities is ~1e9-1e10 builds and a pure-Python neighbor_pairs will not survive. Add a perf task: vectorize neighbor_pairs with numpy, special-case STATIC (build the cell-list ONCE since positions never change), set a concrete wall-clock benchmark, and decide the Rust trigger with a number. Consider scoping 50k to a fast-follow and documenting the v1 target.

Logistic fit by logit-linear least-squares (Task 11) blows up at ratio=0/1, exactly the saturated tails that dominate a cliff. Implement a small pure-numpy Gauss-Newton / LM logistic MLE on per-replication binomial counts (handles 0/1 cleanly) and get the midpoint CI by bootstrapping over reps; add a test that it recovers a synthetic sigmoid midpoint from a mostly-0/1 grid.

Half-duplex / shared-vs-per-direction budget (spec section 3) is unresolved. For BLE half-duplex, charge ONE shared budget per episode split across both directions (not a full budget each way); add a test that total blobs in an episode <= single-episode budget regardless of direction count; justify in README.

n_local_contenders is sampled at contact midpoint (spec section 6.1 says 'at contact time') — a defensible single-snapshot simplification but an unstated abstraction. Record it in the README fidelity-to-parent table with bias direction (optimistic: understates time-varying contention); optionally add entry/midpoint/max-over-episode config for the alpha sensitivity sweep section 6.1 asks for.

Exactly-grazing contact (disc==0 -> None in contact_interval) is neither documented nor tested; the passing tangential test uses offset 1.9 (disc>0). Pin the measure-zero convention with a one-line test so a future disc<0 refactor cannot silently change behavior.
synthesis.goRecommendation: NO-GO until the gate is re-specified. Do NOT start the build. All three independent review lenses returned needs-rework and converged on the same root cause: the percolation gate — the plan's single load-bearing validation that 'gates everything downstream' — is broken in three compounding ways (one-hop engine, P_inf^2-vs-4.51 estimator error, no independent oracle), so it will reject correct code and an implementer driving a green-loop gate will be pressured to hack the acceptance band into a 'confident, reproducible, and wrong' cliff — the exact outcome the spec was revised to prevent. This is the highest-risk item and must be fixed in the plan first. Sequence the rework as: (1) re-specify the gate (multi-hop fixpoint engine + susceptibility-peak/giant-component knee locator + union-find KAT oracle), proving multi-hop and the threshold estimator BEFORE the degree sweep; (2) wire contact_interval into the engine and add the dt-convergence test; (3) fix the Wilson CI to per-replication units; (4) make the RWP stationarity check and empirical-vs-nominal degree real acceptance gates; (5) correct the fair-chance denominator predicate. The TDD spine, dependency DAG, determinism contract, invariant lint, and geometry tests are genuinely sound and worth keeping — this is a fixable plan, not a rewrite, but it is GO only after the seven MUST-FIX items are folded in. Verdict: ready-after-fixes once the gate and its prerequisites are corrected."
